site.tone: A November journey through markets, mountain air, studio visits, and a beautifully slow coast finish — designed to feel culturally rich, visually calm, and easy to use while travelling.

journeySummary.summary: Markets, architecture, maker visits, long lunches, and a sense of being fully inside the city rather than skimming it.
One mountain night for contrast and reset, kept deliberately simple so the middle of the trip feels restorative rather than busy.
A calm coast finish with protected free time, a few lightly programmed moments, and a gentler pace for the final memory of the trip.

stays.name: Oaxaca courtyard hotel
stays.summary: A calm, design-aware city base with easy access to markets, galleries, and dinners on foot.

stays.name: Sierra Norte lodge
stays.summary: Simple, scenic, and chosen for a proper reset: cooler air, one guided walk, and a genuinely early night.

stays.name: Pacific coast apartment retreat
stays.summary: Intimate, warm, and visually calm — chosen so the final days can hold both planned moments and real idleness.
notes: Beach club and dinner suggestions loaded below

signatureMoments.title: Morning market pass
signatureMoments.summary: Start the city with market texture first, before museums or formal touring.

signatureMoments.title: Textile studio conversation
signatureMoments.summary: A small human-scale visit that gives the craft context and keeps the day feeling personal.

signatureMoments.title: Sunset beach and lagoon option
signatureMoments.summary: The trip ends with choices, not obligations: stay horizontal, or add one last nature note if the mood is right.

weather.sun: Best for valley outings and coast days: keep the longer outside lunch, the beach window, and the studio stop. Use hats, light linen, and cash for small craft purchases.
weather.rain: If energy dips or weather turns, swap the more exposed excursion for a gentler museum pass, longer lunch, or apartment terrace afternoon. The point is to preserve tone, not force the original plan.

checklist: Light layers for mountain temperature change
Sandals plus one smarter dinner option
Cash for small producers and market stops
copyNotes: Nos vemos en el patio del hotel a las diez.
Carry some cash for small producers and market stops.

days.title: Arrive into Oaxaca
days.summary: A soft landing into the city: private meet at the airport, check-in at the courtyard hotel, and a first dinner nearby so the trip begins with atmosphere rather than logistics.
timeline: Driver meets at arrivals and takes you directly into Oaxaca Centro.
Check in, unpack, and take the courtyard slowly.
Choose between courtyard dining, refined local cooking, or a rooftop supper depending on energy.

days.title: City orientation and architecture
days.summary: Use the first full day to get your bearings: Santo Domingo, a measured old-town walk, and enough free time to begin forming your own favourites.
timeline: Ease into the city from the hotel courtyard before heading out.
Architecture, plazas, and a first proper sense of route and scale.
A table chosen more for mood than checklist-value.
Return to a gallery, bar, or rooftop if it feels right.

days.title: Culinary Oaxaca
days.summary: A signature food day: market textures in the morning, a hands-on cooking session, then a free late afternoon to browse, rest, or follow a dinner suggestion.
timeline: Walk Mercado 20 de Noviembre before the city gets too hot.
Hands-on lunch prep with regional ingredients and stories.
Take a mezcal bar, gallery, or rooftop option only if it feels right.

days.title: Maker day in the valley
days.summary: A more personal day built around a textile studio conversation, lunch with a producer, and an easy return to the city for the final night in Oaxaca.
timeline: Private driver collects from the courtyard entrance.
A small-scale visit that gives the craft a human frame.
Long lunch in the valley before returning.
Easy evening close to the hotel.

days.title: Sierra Norte reset
days.summary: Just enough time in the mountains to shift the emotional register of the trip: cooler air, a guided walk, and one quiet evening before the coast.
timeline: Driver route north with a light coffee stop.
A low-pressure trail designed for pace and atmosphere, not mileage.
Early supper and a genuinely quiet night.

days.title: Transfer to the coast
days.summary: A travel day handled as smoothly as possible, ending at the coast apartment in time for a reset, a view, and an uncomplicated dinner nearby.
timeline: Travel south with a comfort stop en route.
Check in and settle before the coast section opens up.
Keep the first coast evening light and close by.

days.title: First full coast day
days.summary: The coast begins without over-programming: a protected free day to swim, slow down, and let the final third of the trip breathe properly.
timeline: Take the morning at the apartment terrace or nearby café.
A long middle stretch reserved for actual rest.
Go out only if the mood is there.

days.title: Lagoon or low-key coast day
days.summary: Hold this day open until the weather and mood are clear: take the lagoon option if conditions are right, otherwise keep the rhythm local and easy.
timeline: Choose between the lagoon excursion or a local coast day.
Keep the middle of the day generous and unhurried.
If you go out, be back in time for a calm evening.

days.title: Design-led free day
days.summary: A day deliberately protected from over-scheduling so the coast section feels like a real exhale rather than a second sightseeing sprint.
timeline: Choose the quieter option based on mood.
Use the shortlist only if useful.

days.title: Last full coast day
days.summary: Keep the final full day emotionally light: one last swim, one proper lunch, and a sunset plan worthy of the closing stretch.
timeline: No alarm energy unless you want it.
Make this one of the memorable coast meals.
Beach walk or rooftop depending on weather.

days.title: Departure day
days.summary: A clean departure day with transfer timing handled, checkout simplified, and enough buffer to leave the trip feeling orderly rather than rushed.
timeline: Keep the last morning simple and unhurried.
Driver timing aligned with departure window.
Head to the airport with buffer built in.

studio.productSummary: A private trip-building studio where one trip record powers the proposal, revision history, booked itinerary, and live companion site.
studio.sourceNotes: We want Mexico for late November. Cultured, atmospheric, not overly hectic. A few very well-chosen hotels and apartments. Food, design, local texture, but no over-programmed sightseeing.

workflowSteps.name: 1. Create trip record
body: Start with a structured trip record rather than a blank design canvas so every proposal, revision, and live update stays tied to one source of truth.
The first screen should feel operational: client names, dates, budget band, trip type, privacy level, and chosen template.
modules: Create trip: client name, dates, budget, privacy, mode
Choose template: culture-first / coast finish / family / honeymoon
Generate private slug and working URL immediately
assistant: Summarise the enquiry in one paragraph
Suggest the best-fit template based on tone and season
Draft a first hero headline from the brief

workflowSteps.name: 2. Import enquiry + notes
body: Import the enquiry email, call notes, PDF snippets, and supplier references into one source panel so the raw inputs stop living across inboxes and documents.
Imported text does not publish automatically. It becomes source material for the trip designer to shape.
modules: Paste email, questionnaire, or notes into one parser panel
Extract travellers, dates, budget, style cues, exclusions
Flag gaps before the next call
assistant: Extract structured fields from messy notes

workflowSteps.name: 3. Shape brief + route
body: Turn raw input into trip logic: why this destination, what pace suits them, how many bases is enough, and what the trip should never become.
The software should make these decisions legible, not replace them.
modules: Edit trip brief and 'why this fits' rationale
Set route structure, stay logic, and signature moments
Mark optional experiences versus core anchors
assistant: Stress-test the route against pace preferences
Generate alternate headings for each major section

workflowSteps.name: 4. Generate proposal site
body: Once the route is shaped, generate the first private site from structured modules: hero, route cards, stay cards, itinerary cards, and proposal framing.
This should feel like a signature client experience rather than a brochure PDF.
modules: Pick mode: proposal / revised / booked / live trip
Choose which modules are visible in proposal mode
assistant: Turn structured data into elegant section copy
Produce a concise cover note for the client email

workflowSteps.name: 5. Revise + publish live site
body: Client feedback updates the same trip record. After booking, the operational layer is added: contacts, today plan, packing, transfers, maps, and day-of-use prompts.
That is what turns the microsite into a premium working product rather than a static sales artefact.
modules: Revision log with client-visible change summary
Booked mode adds maps, support, stays, transfers, and full itinerary
Publish live update without rebuilding manually
